front: Composer: student of Ambroise Thomas; Gabriel Fauré criticized his 'vulgar and passionate writhings'
Composer: wrote a comic opera Cherubin as a sequel to Mozart's Marriage of Figaro, with a lyric soprano in the title male role
Composer: cast Sibyl Sanderson in the title role of Esclarmonde specifically to use the high G6 'Eiffel Tower note'
Composer: an aria from his opera Le Cid inspired Laurie Anderson's 1981 performance piece 'O Superman'
Opera: features the 'Meditation,' a D-major entr'acte for solo violin and orchestra marked Andante Religioso, that plays as the title courtesan contemplates converting to Christianity
Opera: the monk Athanael tries to convert the title courtesan to Christianity but discovers his attraction to her is sexual; she eventually joins a convent and is praised as a saint
Opera: title character asks to spare her statue of Eros even as she agrees to destroy all her possessions
Opera: heroine sings 'Adieu, notre petite table' when she decides to leave her lover for Monsieur de Bretigny
Opera: heroine dies in the arms of Chevalier des Grieux before she is to be deported; based on a novel by the Abbé Prévost
Opera: des Grieux sings the 'Dream Song' aria 'En fermant les yeux'
Opera: Act III opens with a letter scene; Charlotte sings 'Va! Laisse couler mes larmes' accompanied by an alto saxophone solo
Opera: title character sings 'Pourquoi me reveiller?' while reciting Ossian's poetry to his married love interest, then asks her husband Albert to lend him two pistols
Opera: begins and ends with the Christmas carol 'Noel! Jesus vient de naitre' sung by the bailiff's children
Opera: based on Goethe's epistolary novel; the title tenor role was adjusted to baritone for Mattia Battistini
Opera: title character, daughter of Byzantine Emperor Phorcas, loves Roland and gives him a sword but conceals her face; Roland wins a tournament to earn the right to marry her
